principle: Separate pattern library from design system — a library captures patterns but the system is the connected patterns plus shared practices; a complete library without shared language still produces duplicated inconsistent interfaces
explanation: X.ui|library_vs_system: design_system=patterns+shared_practices(coherently_organized_for_product_purpose); pattern_library=tool_to_capture+share_patterns NOT_the_system_itself; library_alone: corrects_small_inconsistencies BUT_cannot_fix_bad_design+patterns_still_misused; NO:treating_library_completion_as_system_completion; eg: Sipgate_built_thorough_library -> year_later_still_full_of_duplicated_patterns -> missing_shared_practices_not_missing_docs

principle: Write design principles as testable criteria not aspirational adjectives — 'Simple, useful, enjoyable' applies to every product and guides no decision; a testable principle changes what a designer does next
explanation: X.ui|actionable_principles: vague(simple/useful/enjoyable)=applies_to_everything=guides_nothing; actionable=can_be_tested_on_real_interface; test: can_new_team_member_use_this_to_decide? NO:principles_every_product_already_has; eg: "Make_it_simple" >> "No_needless_parts:_every_element_must_have_purpose_or_be_removed" -> designer_can_audit_element_by_element; "Make_it_clear" >> "Only_one_#1_priority:_what_do_users_see+do_first?"

principle: Rank design principles in explicit priority order — when clarity conflicts with beauty unranked principles create debate; Salesforce Lightning's order Clarity then Efficiency then Consistency then Beauty resolves every conflict automatically
explanation: X.ui|principles_priority: multiple_principles->inevitable_conflicts->explicit_rank_resolves_without_discussion; Salesforce_Lightning: Clarity>Efficiency>Consistency>Beauty_always_in_this_order; Medium_Direction_over_Choice: purposely_fewer_formatting_options->users_focus_on_writing; principles_have_point_of_view+encourage_designers_to_take_perspective; NO:unranked_equal-weight_principles; eg: debate_bold_animation_vs_readable_text -> Clarity_first_rule_resolves_immediately

principle: Keep design principles to three to five — TED, Atlassian, and Airbnb teams recalled theirs instantly without hesitation; principles no one remembers provide no creative direction regardless of how well they are written
explanation: X.ui|principles_count: human_memory_limit->max_4_reliably; optimal=3-5; test: ask_team_members_to_recall_unprompted -> hesitation_or_confusion=principles_not_working; must_be_in_constant_use: design_critiques+presentations+daily_conversations; NO:10+_principles_displayed_on_wall_nobody_references; eg: 8_abstract_principles >> 4_principles_everyone_recalls+applies_daily -> design_decisions_align_without_meetings

principle: Pair each design principle with a specific interface example — abstract wording is interpreted differently by each person; a real screen showing the principle in action aligns the team's understanding of what the principle actually means
explanation: X.ui|principles_anchored: even_best-worded_principle_is_interpreted_differently; fix: find_specific_screen_where_principle_is_clearly_demonstrated -> connect_them; can_you_point_to_page_showing_"only_one_#1_priority"?; NO:principles_without_concrete_interface_illustration; eg: TED_"Timeless_not_cutting-edge" -> show_page_without_parallax_despite_trend -> team_understands_what_timeless_means_for_THIS_product_specifically

principle: Name interface patterns with verbs that describe user actions not nouns that describe appearance — 'Course banner' limits the pattern to one content type; 'Billboard' which promotes anything unlocks reuse across courses events and articles

Run interface inventories by printing all screens and grouping cut-out elements by purpose — this reveals you have twelve button variants and seven header styles; without inventory you are guessing at the real state of your own system
explanation: X.ui|interface_inventory: process: print_all_screens->cut_out_elements->group_by_purpose(navigation/forms/buttons/lists)->label_groups; reveals: duplicates+inconsistencies+missing_patterns; run: first_inventory=comprehensive; then_every_few_months(2-3h_each); NO:building_pattern_library_without_inventory_first; eg: team_assumes_4_button_styles >> inventory_reveals_17_variants -> consolidation_decisions_based_on_real_data_not_assumptions

principle: Draw a module's content structure before designing it visually — agree on required elements optional elements and hierarchy with the full team; this aligns design and development intent before any visual decisions commit anyone
explanation: X.ui|content_structure_first: before_visual_design: list_required_elements+decide_hierarchy+grouping+sketch_structure; eg_Billboard: heading(required)+strong_CTA(required)+background(required)+label(optional); NOT_a_wireframe -> focused_on_content_hierarchy_specifically; designer+developer+content_work_in_parallel_once_structure_agreed; NO:starting_visual_comp_before_content_agreement; eg: promo_module_debate -> 5min_structure_sketch -> 2hrs_parallel_design+dev_work

principle: Group interface elements by user behavior they support before systemizing them visually — 'Refine a list' uses tabs on some pages and dropdowns on others; behavior-based grouping exposes consolidation candidates that appearance-based grouping hides
explanation: X.ui|behaviors_before_visual: wrong: group_by_visual_type_first; right: identify_behaviors(Discovery+Catalog+Wishlist)->break_into_actions->find_elements_per_action->group_by_purpose; reveals: same_action_solved_by_different_elements=consolidation_candidate; NO:grouping_components_only_by_visual_type_or_page_location; eg: "refine_list"_behavior->tabs_some_pages+dropdown_others -> one_canonical_pattern_identified

principle: Separate functional patterns from perceptual patterns in your system — functional patterns support user actions and break usability when changed; perceptual patterns carry brand feel and break coherence; they evolve on different timescales and need different governance

Brand identity lives in the proportions and relationships between perceptual elements not in individual elements — TED red used sparingly is TED; too much red stops feeling like TED; the ratio matters as much as the color value itself
explanation: X.ui|brand_proportions: brand_feel=relationships+proportions_between_elements NOT_individual_elements; how_colors_relate+how_imagery_relates_to_typography+how_typography_relates_to_spacing; TED: "red_used_sparingly+thoughtfully; wrong_place_or_too_much->doesn't_feel_like_TED"; Spotify_intimacy: right_ratio_green_to_black+subdued_imagery+subtle_interactions together; NO:defining_brand_only_by_hex_color_values; eg: brand_color_applied_everywhere >> proportion_rules_defined -> brand_feel_restored

principle: Perfect consistency produces generic design — a system enforcing uniformity everywhere erodes the distinctive qualities that make a brand recognizable; reserve creative freedom in brand expression areas while keeping functional UI consistent
explanation: X.ui|consistency_paradox: fine_line: consistency(effective_repeating_patterns) vs uniformity(mechanically_identical_everywhere); FutureLearn: over-systematized_courses_page -> more_consistent BUT_lost_visual_distinctiveness; rule: strict_consistency_in_functional_UI + experimentation_allowed_in_marketing+campaign_areas; NO:same_strict_rules_on_utility_screens_and_brand_screens; eg: fully_uniform_product >> marketing_pages_allowed_brand_experiments -> system_stays_alive+distinctive

principle: Invest in signature moments — small interactions with meaning become product differentiators; TED's play button ripple echoes their video drop animation; a single signature moment feels disconnected until it is echoed elsewhere throughout the interface

Define brand personality with 5 to 7 traits plus explicit traits to avoid — MailChimp's 'Fun but not childish, powerful but not complicated' prevents more design mistakes than a list of positive traits alone ever could
explanation: X.ui|brand_persona_traits: 5-7_traits+explicit_opposing_limits; paired_format(positive+negative): prevents_overreach_into_adjacent_wrong_territory; MailChimp: Fun_but_not_childish+Funny_but_not_goofy+Powerful_but_not_complicated+Hip_but_not_alienating+Informal_but_not_sloppy; derive_visual_lexicon_from_traits: bright_slightly_desaturated_palette+simple_sans_serif+flat_elements_with_soft_textures; NO:traits_without_explicit_opposing_limits; eg: "friendly" >> "friendly_but_not_unprofessional" -> distinct_visual_boundaries

principle: Naming components by appearance causes library fragmentation — teams cannot find 'Prominent tile' so they create a new one; a year of enthusiastic documentation produces a system still full of duplicates but now they are all thoroughly named
explanation: X.ui|presentational_naming_antipattern: presentation_names(Prominent_tile/Circle_with_dot): unfindable_as_library_grows+no_guidance_where_to_use; Sipgate: thorough_docs+presentation_names -> year_later_too_many_modules -> easier_to_add_new_than_find_existing; NO:naming_by_color(blue_button) NO:naming_by_position(top_left_module) NO:naming_by_size(large_card); eg: "Secondary_promotional_tile"(forgotten) >> "Spotlight"(recalled+reused_across_contexts)

principle: Effective component names are metaphorical have personality or communicate purpose — the name must create a mental image; if no image forms the pattern will be forgotten and recreated rather than reused from the library
